findings.location: N/A - no mention of TurboTax Free product
findings.reasoning: The rule triggers when 'TurboTax Free' is mentioned on the page. Scanning the full page text, there is no mention of 'TurboTax Free', 'Free Edition', or any free product offering. The page mentions general TurboTax products/services (e.g., 'TurboTax expert', 'file for you', links to 'Free Edition tax filing' in the footer navigation menu), but the footer navigation is a generic site-wide menu of links to various product pages, not a marketing mention/claim about the Free product on this page itself. The footer link text 'Free Edition tax filing' is simply a navigational link label, not a promotional claim or mention that would trigger a disclosure requirement under R-01. The body content of the article is about general money management tips and does not reference TurboTax Free at all. Strongest counter-reading: one could argue the footer nav link 'Free Edition tax filing' counts as a 'mention' of TurboTax Free triggering the rule. However, this is a standard site-wide navigation menu present on all blog pages, not a substantive marketing mention of the free product's value proposition or eligibility - the rule's intent is to require disclosure when Free is actively promoted/marketed to consumers, not when it's merely one of many links in a global footer sitemap. Treating every footer nav link as a trigger would require this disclosure on literally every page site-wide regardless of content, which is not a reasonable reading of the rule's intent. Therefore I conclude trigger not met.

Reviewed the full page text, including body, sidebar, CTAs, navigation, and footer links. The page references general TurboTax products and a "Free Edition tax filing" link in the footer navigation, but it does not explicitly mention "TurboTax Free" or characterize the product as free within the page content itself. Rule R-01 is scoped to situations where TurboTax Free is mentioned in the marketing copy, which does not occur here. A strict reading would not treat a bare navigational link label as a triggered marketing claim requiring the specific disclosure underneath. Therefore, the rule’s IF-condition is not met, and the rule is not applicable to this page.

findings.location: shared footer – Online Software Products navigation
findings.reasoning: Trigger is met because the page explicitly mentions TurboTax’s Free product via the “Free Edition tax filing” link in the footer. Under R-01, this requires the eligibility disclosure to appear right underneath. No disclosure text about “~37% of filers qualify” or “Simple Form 1040 returns only…” appears underneath this mention (or elsewhere on the page). Therefore Axis A is not compliant, and since there is no disclosure text at all, Axis B does not match the approved library entry. Strongest counter-reading: a generic footer navigation link might not count as a promotional mention. However, precedent and the worked example treat shared footer mentions as triggering, so the rule applies here.
